Task id: T83 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Base commit: d0f8a82 (main) — your worktree may be cut from an older HEAD; FIRST run `git reset --hard d0f8a82` inside YOUR worktree (sanctioned base-refresh), then `git switch -c implement/T83` so your commit lands on the task branch.

NOTE: this is a Go repository (wanbond). The check gate is `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c` if go is not on PATH (per AGENTS.md); there is no bun here. This is a BEHAVIOR-PRESERVING structural refactor — keep every existing test body passing unmodified (constructor plumbing changes only where unavoidable).

headline: Introduce peerState and key Multipath state by peer

description: In internal/bind, introduce a peerState type holding the fields that are singletons today: virt (*udpEndpoint), outerSeq, scheduler, fecSend/fecRecv, resequencer (atomic.Pointer), reflector, sendCodec, the peer's path/remote view, and probers. ALSO split pathState (multipath.go:81) into SHARED per-socket state (name, id, src, conn, readLoop, deferred-path machinery) and per-(peer,path) state (codec, remote/hasRemote, prober, txBytes/rxBytes) — concentrator sockets are SHARED across peers while each peer owns its return-path/remote view, so the decomposition must be explicit. RUNTIME PATH MUTATION IS IN SCOPE (R72): the live dynamic-path machinery (deferred paths, internal/bind/runtime_path_test.go, tolerant_membership_test.go) operates on the SHARED socket state, so adding or removing a shared path at runtime must instantiate/tear down the per-(peer,path) state (codec, remote/hasRemote, prober, txBytes/rxBytes) for EVERY currently-bound peer — design the split so this fan-out has a single owner and is exercised while >=2 peerStates exist. Change Multipath to hold the shared path list plus a map keyed by peer id/name plus the lookup maps needed later (endpoint->peer, source->peer placeholders). Construct EXACTLY ONE peerState on the single-peer path so behavior is byte-identical. Preserve the m.mu discipline and the lock-free receive fast path (resequencer/fecRecv stay atomic.Pointer per peer). Keep the conn seam isolated to internal/bind/bind.go.

acceptance: `go build ./...` succeeds and the full existing internal/bind test suite passes unchanged (single-peer path proven behavior-preserving) — including the runtime-path suites (runtime_path_test.go, tolerant_membership_test.go). The former singleton fields are now reached through peerState; a grep shows no remaining process-global resequencer/outerSeq/scheduler on Multipath; per-(peer,path) state (remote/prober/tx/rx) is held off the shared socket state. A new unit test asserts that with two peerStates bound, adding a shared path at runtime creates per-(peer,path) state for BOTH peers and removing it tears down both peers' per-(peer,path) state, leaving each peer's remaining paths untouched.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. - cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.

- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". - keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

# Agent instructions — wanbond

Instructions for AI agents (and humans) working in this repo. Read the
[README](README.md) for what wanbond is and [docs/design.md](docs/design.md) for
the architecture before changing anything.

## Keep the docs current (required)

**Documentation is part of the definition of done.** When a change alters
behavior, configuration, architecture, the wire format, an invariant, or an
operational step, update the affected docs **in the same change**:

- **[README.md](README.md)** — the front door: capabilities, quick start, repo
  layout, status/limitations. Update when a capability, config surface, package,
  or limitation changes.
- **[docs/design.md](docs/design.md)** — architecture and what we built on top of
  amneziawg-go. Update when the data path, a layer/package, an invariant, the
  security model, or a deliberate boundary changes.
- **[docs/install.md](docs/install.md)** — setup and operation. Update when
  config keys, systemd units, firewall/provisioning steps, or metrics change.
- **[docs/manual-checklist.md](docs/manual-checklist.md)** — update when a
  verification step or a real-link measurement changes.

A change that moves the code but not the docs it invalidates is **incomplete**.
If you are unsure whether a doc is affected, grep it for the symbol/keyword you
touched. Prefer editing the existing doc over adding a new one; keep docs
consistent with each other (e.g. a config key described in README, design, and
install must match).

## Build, test, lint

The dev shell (`nix develop`) puts Go 1.26, golangci-lint, and the netem/DPI
tooling on `PATH`. Run tools from it (e.g. `nix develop --command bash -c '…'`).

```sh
just build      # go build ./...
just test       # unprivileged unit/property tests (go test ./...)
just lint       # go vet + golangci-lint, INCLUDING -tags e2e and -tags realhosts
just e2e        # privileged netns fixture: sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
just realhosts  # opt-in real-machine tier (-tags realhosts), report-only
```

The non-privileged gate a change must pass before merge:

```sh
go build ./... && go vet ./... && test -z "$(gofmt -l cmd internal test)" && go test ./...
```

`-tags e2e` / `-tags realhosts` need root / real hosts and are **not** part of the
default gate; validate them separately (see Testing discipline).

## Load-bearing invariants — do not break

Full detail in [docs/design.md §Load-bearing invariants](docs/design.md). In
short:

1. The engine sees **one virtual endpoint per peer**; the Bind fans out beneath
   it (design rule A1). No per-packet endpoint churn to the engine.
2. Use wanbond's **own outer sequence space**; never reuse/perturb the inner
   WireGuard counter.
3. **Resequence before** the inner anti-replay window validates.
4. Inner fail-closed; **PROBE/CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated** with monotonic
   anti-replay; DATA/PARITY are unauthenticated **by design** (DoS-grade risk
   accepted — do not "fix" this without a design decision).
5. All engine (`conn`) coupling stays isolated to **`internal/bind/bind.go`** —
   preserve the fork-swap hedge.
6. Amnezia config is **all-or-nothing** and the engine is **single-per-process**
   (package-level globals). Keep the config validation that enforces this.
7. On **any `klauspost/reedsolomon` (or amneziawg-go) version bump**, re-verify
   `TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant` (`internal/fec`) before landing —
   a flipped default matrix silently corrupts every reconstructed payload.

## Testing discipline

- **Reproduce before fixing.** For a suspected defect, write a failing test (or a
  documented minimal repro) that fails for the *expected* reason *first*; then fix
  and confirm it passes. Assertions must be non-vacuous — a test that cannot fail
  on the unfixed code proves nothing (e.g. a goroutine-leak fix needs a
  `goleak`/`NumGoroutine` gate, not a bare `-count` run).
- **The netns fixture is CPU/PPS-bound.** It validates *functional* bonding,

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"crypto/rand"
5		"errors"
6		"fmt"
7		"net"
8		"net/netip"
9		"sync"
10		"sync/atomic"
11		"syscall"
12		"time"
13	
14		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/conn"
15	
16		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"
17		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
18		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
19		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
20		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/reseq"
21		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
22		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
23	)
24	
25	// socketRecvBuffer is the SO_RCVBUF requested on every per-path socket, matching
26	// wireguard-go's StdNetBind (~7 MiB). A large receive buffer absorbs the bursts a
27	// bonded, reordered flow delivers while the userspace engine catches up; the
28	// pass-through P0 Bind used the OS default and lost datagrams under load (P0
29	// findings §1/§2). The kernel silently caps the request at net.core.rmem_max, so
30	// the effective size may be smaller on an untuned host — best-effort by design.
31	const socketRecvBuffer = 7 << 20
32	
33	// multipathBatchSize is the number of datagrams a ReceiveFunc / Send handles per
34	// call. T12 keeps it at 1 (one syscall per datagram, per path); GSO/GRO batching
35	// is best-effort future work (P0 findings §2) tracked separately and does not
36	// change the wire format.
37	const multipathBatchSize = 1
38	
39	// maxDatagram bounds a single received outer datagram. It comfortably exceeds a
40	// full-MTU inner packet plus every outer/WG/amnezia-junk overhead.
41	const maxDatagram = 65535
42	
43	// Receive-resequencer tuning (T18), sized against P0 findings §6.
44	//
45	// resequencerWindow is the maximum span of outer-seq positions the receive
46	// resequencer buffers while it waits for an out-of-order frame. WG's inner RFC
47	// 6479 anti-replay window is 8128 messages (docs/p0-findings.md §6); the window
48	// here sits ~4x below that, so even a worst-case burst of reordered/held frames
49	// stays comfortably inside the inner filter's tolerance while spanning far more
50	// packets than the measured ~19 ms emulated cross-path skew (and the larger,
51	// more variable real Starlink/5G skew) needs at realistic per-path rates.
52	//
53	// resequencerTimeout bounds how long a head-of-line-blocked run is held for a
54	// missing (presumed-lost) lower frame before that gap is skipped and the run
55	// released. It caps the added latency — and the stall a slow/lossy path can
56	// impose on the whole bond (P0 §7 head-of-line concern) — to a few multiples of
57	// a Starlink RTT (~45 ms) rather than holding a gap forever.
58	const (
59		resequencerWindow  = 2048
60		resequencerTimeout = 250 * time.Millisecond
61	)

67		// outage. It is DISTINCT from errNoHealthyPath so operator logs and the e2e
68		// log-grep harness can tell shedding from total path failure (the drop behavior is

75	// pathState is one configured uplink: its source-bound UDP socket, stable
76	// path-id, its own decode Codec (each path receives on its own goroutine, so the
77	// Codec's scratch is never shared), and the per-path remote endpoint. The remote
78	// is either configured (edge dest_addr / peer endpoint) or LEARNED from inbound
79	// traffic (concentrator) — but it lives strictly BELOW the engine's single
80	// virtual endpoint, so the engine never sees this per-path bookkeeping churn.
81	type pathState struct {
82		name  string
83		id    uint8
84		src   netip.Addr
85		conn  *net.UDPConn
86		codec *frame.Codec
87		// prober is this path's own probe initiator (nil when the bind runs without the
88		// probe transport). It is set at path creation and immutable for the path's life,
89		// so the Bind-owned receive goroutine reaches it via the pathState it already
90		// holds — NOT through a shared, dynamically-mutated m.probers slice — which is
91		// what keeps echo handling race-free while paths are added/removed at runtime (T30).
92		prober *telemetry.Prober
93	
94		// txBytes/rxBytes are cumulative OUTER-wire byte counters for this path, the
95		// per-path traffic accounting the /metrics exposition reports (T23). txBytes counts
96		// the DATA-frame wire bytes this path egresses on the Send hot path; rxBytes counts
97		// every outer datagram this path's readLoop receives (DATA, PROBE, and echo alike —
98		// the true received wire volume). They are atomics so the send/receive hot paths
99		// increment them WITHOUT taking m.mu (lock-free), and the scrape/snapshot path reads
100		// them with a plain atomic Load; a single writer per counter (Send holds m.mu while
101		// choosing the path, and each path has exactly one readLoop) means the Add is
102		// uncontended in the common case. "Bytes on Starlink vs 5G" — the data-thrift signal
103		// (requirement 2) — is exactly txBytes: when the weighted scheduler collapses to the
104		// primary under sub-capacity load, the backup path's Send count stays ~flat.
105		txBytes atomic.Uint64
106		rxBytes atomic.Uint64
107	
108		mu        sync.Mutex
109		remote    netip.AddrPort
110		hasRemote bool
111	}

113	// deferredPath is a configured path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr was not yet
114	// assignable at Open (net.ListenUDP -> EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the
115	// address, e.g. a 5G modem with no DHCP lease at boot). Rather than tear the whole
116	// bond down, Open records the path here instead of binding it: the tunnel comes up
117	// on the paths that DID bind, and this path stays DOWN — its prober, never fed an
118	// echo, reports StateDown, which the scheduler excludes from Pick, exactly as the
119	// runtime path-down model treats a live-but-silent path. It carries the boot-time
120	// prober so the T55 background reconcile that retries the bind reuses the SAME
121	// path-id stamp instead of minting a new one. prober is nil only on a bind without
122	// the probe transport, which never defers (see Open).

124		def    config.Path
125		prober *telemetry.Prober
126	}

140	// Multipath is the P1 bonding conn.Bind: one UDP socket per configured path
141	// (bound to the path's source address), all fronted by a SINGLE stable virtual
142	// endpoint the engine holds per peer. Send wraps each opaque WireGuard datagram
143	// in an outer DATA frame (own outer-seq + path-id) and picks a path; one
144	// Bind-owned reader per path unwraps DATA frames into the shared resequencer, and
145	// a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc drains it and hands the inner WG datagram up
146	// under the shared virtual endpoint (the fan-in that lets paths be added/removed at
147	// runtime without the engine spawning receive goroutines — T30). It replaces
148	// bind.Passthrough behind the same conn.Bind seam (device wiring is unchanged).
149	//
150	// Lifecycle: the sockets live for the duration of an Open→Close span, NOT the
151	// Multipath's whole life. The amneziawg engine calls Close() before every Open()
152	// (device.upLocked → BindUpdate → closeBindLocked, and on IpcSet listen_port /
153	// route-change events) and cycles Close↔Open on Down/Up, so — exactly like
154	// conn.StdNetBind — Open creates the per-path sockets and Close tears them down
155	// AND clears the path state so the next Open rebuilds from scratch. The "closed"
156	// state is simply "no bound sockets" (len(paths)==0); there is no separate sticky
157	// flag that a later Open would have to reset.
158	//
159	// Path policy: an injected sched.Scheduler (T15) chooses the egress path per
160	// datagram. The P1 MVP wires an active-backup scheduler (all traffic on the
161	// preferred primary, transparent failover to a backup on a T13 path-DOWN signal
162	// with failback hysteresis); the weighted/FEC-aware policy (T21) is a different
163	// Scheduler swapped in here with no Bind change. No FEC/resequencing yet
164	// (P2/P3), but every DATA frame carries its outer-seq and path-id so T18/T24 can
165	// consume them.
166	// ProberFactory mints a *telemetry.Prober for a path admitted at runtime (T30),
167	// stamped with the given stable path-id. The device wires it capturing the SAME
168	// per-boot session id, prober config, clock, PSK, and logger the boot-time probers
169	// were built with, so a runtime path's liveness is measured identically to a
170	// boot-time path (its probes join the same session so the peer's reflector adopts
171	// them without a challenge reset). It is nil on a bind built without the probe
172	// transport (the T12 unit tests), which therefore cannot add paths at runtime.
173	type ProberFactory func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober
174	
175	type Multipath struct {
176		psk       config.Key
177		defs      []config.Path
178		scheduler sched.Scheduler
179	
180		// classify maps each outbound datagram to its pacer traffic class from the inner
181		// WireGuard message type, parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia obfuscation profile
182		// so a WireGuard control frame (handshake/keepalive) is recognised — and pacing-
183		// exempted — under advanced security too, not only in vanilla mode (defect D22). It
184		// is immutable after construction and holds no lock, so Send reads it off m.mu.
185		classify wgClassifier
186	
187		// probers is the BOOT-TIME per-path probe initiator set, in path order, shared
188		// with the scheduler (the SAME *telemetry.Prober values back its PathHealth). At
189		// Open each is bound onto its pathState (ps.prober), and thereafter the hot paths
190		// reach a path's prober through the pathState — never by indexing this slice — so
191		// a runtime path add/remove cannot race echo handling. It is nil when the bind
192		// runs without the probe transport (the T12 unit tests, which drive selection via
193		// AlwaysUp); a nil here also gates the whole probe loop off.
194		probers []*telemetry.Prober
195		// newProber mints a prober for a path admitted at runtime (T30); nil disables
196		// runtime path addition (a bind without the probe transport).
197		newProber ProberFactory
198		// deferredListen binds a reconciled deferred path's socket (T55 background
199		// reconcile). It is an injection seam: the default pins the source IP (net.ListenUDP,
200		// matching AddPath's runtime bind), and a test overrides it to drive the
201		// deferred→bound transition deterministically — a source_addr "becoming assignable"
202		// — without a real interface address having to appear on the host. Immutable after
203		// construction, never nil.
204		deferredListen func(src netip.Addr, port uint16) (*net.UDPConn, error)
205		// reflector answers inbound peer probes (IsEcho=false) with an authenticated
206		// echo. A single Reflector serves every path (its anti-replay is PathID-keyed)
207		// and it is internally synchronized, so the per-path receive goroutines share it.
208		reflector *telemetry.Reflector
209	
210		mu        sync.Mutex
211		paths     []*pathState
212		sendCodec *frame.Codec
213		virt      *udpEndpoint
214		// deferred holds the configured paths whose well-formed source_addr was not yet
215		// assignable at the last Open (EADDRNOTAVAIL). They are NOT in m.paths or the
216		// scheduler — the tunnel runs on the paths that bound — but are recorded here,
217		// index-independent of m.paths, for the T55 background reconcile to retry as their
218		// addresses appear. Rebuilt from scratch on every Open; guarded by m.mu.
219		deferred []deferredPath
220		// defaultRemote is the fallback per-path remote (the peer's wireguard
221		// endpoint) applied to any path without its own dest_addr. It may be set by
222		// ParseEndpoint BEFORE Open, so it is stored here and applied at Open time.
223		defaultRemote    netip.AddrPort
224		hasDefaultRemote bool
225	
226		outerSeq atomic.Uint64
227	
228		// resequencer is the shared T18 receive resequencing buffer. Every path's
229		// decoded DATA frame is pushed through it (by outer-seq) and delivered up the
230		// WG path IN ORDER, so WG's inner anti-replay window never sees multipath
231		// reorder. It is (re)created per Open — matching the socket lifecycle, and
232		// re-pinning its release point after a reconnect — and published atomically so
233		// the per-path receive goroutines read it WITHOUT m.mu, preserving T12's
234		// lock-free receive fast path. Its own mutex is disjoint from m.mu, so it adds
235		// no contention to Send and is never held across a syscall. PROBE handling
236		// (T37) never touches it.
237		resequencer atomic.Pointer[reseq.Resequencer]
238	
239		// FEC datapath (T24). fecCfg is the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon configuration, nil
240		// when FEC is disabled — in which case the whole plane is inert and the datapath
241		// is byte-for-byte the pre-T24 behaviour. fecSend (the send-side encoder + parity
242		// counters) is (re)built per Open and accessed only under m.mu — on the Send path
243		// and the single deadline-tick goroutine (which TryLocks). fecRecv (the receive-
244		// side recovery decoder, self-guarded) is published atomically like resequencer so
245		// the per-path readLoop goroutines read it WITHOUT m.mu, preserving the lock-free
246		// receive fast path. Both are nil/empty when FEC is off. See fec.go.
247		fecCfg  *fec.Config
248		fecSend *fecSender
249		fecRecv atomic.Pointer[fecReceiver]
250	
251		// adaptiveCfg is the adaptive-FEC controller configuration (T29), nil in fixed-ratio
252		// mode. When both fecCfg and adaptiveCfg are non-nil, Open builds one adaptivefec
253		// Controller per span and attaches it to fecSend; the FEC tick loop then drives it
254		// from the measured per-path loss and resizes the encoder's per-group parity. It is
255		// immutable after construction, so the send/receive hot paths read it without a lock.
256		adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config
257	
258		// Receive fan-in (T30). To let a path be added at runtime WITHOUT the engine
259		// spawning a new receive goroutine (it only builds its receive goroutines once,
260		// from the ReceiveFuncs Open returns), the per-path socket reads are decoupled
261		// from the engine-facing delivery: the Bind owns one readLoop goroutine PER path
262		// (tracked by readersWG) that reads its socket and feeds the shared resequencer,
263		// and Open returns a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc that drains the resequencer
264		// in order. A reader pokes deliverSignal after each datagram so the drainer wakes;
265		// recvClosed is closed by Close to release both the drainer and any reader parked
266		// on a delivery. openPort mirrors Open's bind port so a runtime-added path binds
267		// consistently; nextPathID is a monotonic high-water that persists ACROSS Open
268		// spans (a Close->Open never lowers it; only a process restart resets it), so a
269		// surviving path is never renumbered and a freed id is never reused for the
270		// process lifetime, and thus can never collide with the peer's per-path reflector
271		// state.
272		deliverSignal chan struct{}
273		recvClosed    chan struct{}
274		readersWG     sync.WaitGroup
275		openPort      uint16
276		nextPathID    uint16
277	
278		// Receive-path liveness sweep (T39, defect D15). Liveness DOWN-detection normally
279		// rides StartProbeLoop's single wall-clock ticker goroutine (emitProbes → Tick).
280		// Under heavy CPU load — e.g. the concentrator absorbing a saturating forward flood
281		// on 4 vCPU — that timer goroutine can be scheduled with ~1s jitter, delaying a
282		// path-DOWN transition (and thus the reply-direction failover) past the P1 budget.
283		// The per-path receive goroutines, in contrast, are the goroutines the inbound
284		// traffic is ALREADY scheduling, so driving Tick from them re-evaluates liveness
285		// even when the timer goroutine is starved. sweepIntervalNanos is the probe
286		// interval (0 until StartProbeLoop arms it — a bind without the probe transport
287		// never sweeps); lastSweepNanos is the wall-clock high-water throttling the sweep
288		// to at most once per interval so the receive hot path stays cheap.
289		sweepIntervalNanos atomic.Int64
290		lastSweepNanos     atomic.Int64
291	}

293	// compile-time proof that Multipath satisfies the engine's Bind contract.
294	var _ Bind = (*Multipath)(nil)
295	
296	// NewMultipath returns a closed multipath Bind over the configured paths; call
297	// Open to bind the per-path sockets. The PSK keys the outer framing and must be
298	// set (config validation guarantees it). The scheduler is the injected send-side
299	// path-selection policy (T15) whose priority order MUST match the paths slice
300	// order (index 0 = the preferred primary); it is a required collaborator so the
301	// send path is never without a policy.
302	//
303	// probers is the per-path probe initiator that drives on-wire liveness (T13/T37).
304	// When non-nil it MUST hold exactly one *telemetry.Prober per path, in path order,
305	// and those SAME values MUST be the scheduler's PathHealth sources so the liveness
306	// the probe loop measures is the liveness the scheduler selects on. Pass nil to
307	// run the bind without the probe transport (the T12 unit tests drive selection via
308	// sched.AlwaysUp instead). A Reflector is built from the PSK to answer peer probes.
309	//
310	// newProber is the factory the runtime path-add path (AddPath, T30) uses to mint a
311	// prober for a newly-admitted path; it must be non-nil to allow AddPath and MUST be
312	// paired with a non-nil probers (the boot-time set) — a factory without a boot-time
313	// slice would let AddPath append to a nil m.probers, breaking the m.paths/m.probers
314	// alignment invariant and panicking on the next Open at m.probers[i]. Pass nil to
315	// forbid runtime path addition.
316	//
317	// fecCfg is the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon FEC configuration (T24). Pass nil to run the
318	// datapath with FEC disabled (the pre-T24 behaviour, byte-for-byte); a non-nil,
319	// pre-validated config turns the send-side parity encoder and receive-side recovery
320	// decoder on. It is validated here (fail fast) so an invalid ratio is rejected at
321	// construction rather than at the first Open.
322	// adaptiveCfg is the adaptive-FEC controller configuration (T29). Pass nil for the
323	// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24); a non-nil config REQUIRES a non-nil fecCfg (the
324	// controller resizes the FEC encoder's parity — it is meaningless with the plane off)
325	// and reinterprets fecCfg.ParityShards as the controller's parity ceiling. It is
326	// validated here (fail fast) so a mis-tuned control law is rejected at construction.
327	//
328	// amnezia is the tunnel's AmneziaWG obfuscation profile (config.Amnezia). It
329	// parameterizes the send-path frame-type classifier so a WireGuard control frame is
330	// recognised and pacing-exempted under advanced security — custom magic headers and
331	// handshake junk prefixes — as well as in vanilla mode (defect D22). Pass the zero value
332	// for a vanilla (unobfuscated) tunnel; the classifier then uses the default type words
333	// and no junk prefix. It does NOT need to match the config's validated state — an
334	// all-zero profile is exactly the vanilla classifier.
335	func NewMultipath(paths []config.Path, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler, probers []*telemetry.Prober, newProber ProberFactory, fecCfg *fec.Config, adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config, amnezia config.Amnezia) (*Multipath, error) {
336		if len(paths) == 0 {
337			return nil, errors.New("bind: at least one path is required")
338		}
339		if !psk.IsSet() {
340			return nil, errors.New("bind: PSK is required for outer framing")
341		}
342		if len(paths) > 256 {
343			// path-id is a uint8 in the DATA frame header.
344			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: at most 256 paths supported, got %d", len(paths))
345		}
346		if scheduler == nil {
347			return nil, errors.New("bind: a send scheduler is required")
348		}
349		if newProber != nil && probers == nil {
350			// A runtime-path factory without a boot-time prober slice would let AddPath append
351			// to a nil m.probers, desyncing m.paths from m.probers and panicking on the next
352			// Open at m.probers[i]. The two are paired by construction; enforce it here.
353			return nil, errors.New("bind: newProber requires a non-nil probers (boot-time set)")
354		}
355		if probers != nil && len(probers) != len(paths) {
356			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: probers must have one entry per path (got %d, want %d)", len(probers), len(paths))
357		}
358		for i, pr := range probers {
359			if pr == nil {
360				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: prober %d is nil", i)
361			}
362		}
363		if fecCfg != nil {
364			if err := fecCfg.Validate(); err != nil {
365				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: invalid FEC configuration: %w", err)
366			}
367			if fecCfg.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
368				// A deadline this large makes every deadline-flushed group's recovery
369				// structurally late: the resequencer skips the gap (resequencerTimeout) before
370				// the parity-derived frames land, so recovery never delivers. Reject it rather
371				// than ship a bond whose FEC silently cannot help (defect #4).
372				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: fec deadline %s exceeds the max %s (must stay safely below the resequencer's %s per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped)", fecCfg.Deadline, maxFECDeadline, resequencerTimeout)
373			}
374		}
375		if adaptiveCfg != nil {
376			if fecCfg == nil {
377				return nil, errors.New("bind: adaptive FEC requires a FEC configuration (the controller resizes the FEC encoder's parity)")
378			}
379			if err := adaptiveCfg.Validate(); err != nil {
380				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: invalid adaptive FEC configuration: %w", err)
381			}
382			// The controller's parity ceiling (MaxParity) is the fixed FEC ParityShards both
383			// ends agree on: the receiver's decoder is built at fecCfg.ParityShards, and the
384			// encoder must never emit more parity than that ceiling (SetParity clamps to it).
385			// A mismatch would let the controller target a parity index the decoder rejects,
386			// so bind the two together at construction rather than trusting the caller.
387			if adaptiveCfg.MaxParity != fecCfg.ParityShards {
388				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: adaptive FEC parity ceiling (MaxParity=%d) must equal the FEC parity_shards (%d), which the receiver's decoder is built at", adaptiveCfg.MaxParity, fecCfg.ParityShards)
389			}
390			if adaptiveCfg.DataShards != fecCfg.DataShards {
391				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: adaptive FEC DataShards (%d) must equal the FEC data_shards (%d)", adaptiveCfg.DataShards, fecCfg.DataShards)
392			}
393		}
394		return &Multipath{
395			psk:            psk,
396			defs:           append([]config.Path(nil), paths...),
397			scheduler:      scheduler,
398			classify:       newWGClassifier(amnezia),
399			probers:        probers,
400			newProber:      newProber,
401			deferredListen: defaultDeferredListen,
402			reflector:      telemetry.NewReflector(psk, rand.Reader),
403			virt:           &udpEndpoint{},
404			fecCfg:         fecCfg,
405			adaptiveCfg:    adaptiveCfg,
406		}, nil
407	}
408	
409	// Open binds one UDP socket per configured path to that path's source address on
410	// port (0 = random per socket), sets a large SO_RCVBUF on each, spawns one
411	// Bind-owned reader per path, and returns a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc (the
412	// fan-in drainer) plus the first path's bound port. Each path whose config carries
413	// a dest_addr — or, failing that, the peer's wireguard endpoint learned via
414	// ParseEndpoint — starts with a known remote; the rest are learned from inbound
415	// traffic.
416	//
417	// Open is the sole creator of the per-path sockets: the engine's bring-up path
418	// calls Close() first (on the still-unopened bind) and then Open(), so building
419	// the sockets HERE — not in NewMultipath — is what makes that Close→Open sequence
420	// (and every subsequent Down→Up) work. The engine passes the previously-bound
421	// port back on a re-Open; each path binds to it on its own distinct source
422	// address, and the first path's bound port is returned so the engine keeps a
423	// stable listen port across the cycle (matching conn.StdNetBind).
424	func (m *Multipath) Open(port uint16) ([]ReceiveFunc, uint16, error) {
425		m.mu.Lock()
426		defer m.mu.Unlock()
427		if len(m.paths) != 0 {
428			return nil, 0, conn.ErrBindAlreadyOpen
429		}
430	
431		sendCodec, err := frame.NewCodec(m.psk)
432		if err != nil {
433			return nil, 0, err
434		}
435	
436		// A fresh resequencer per Open: its release point re-pins to the first frame
437		// received after this bring-up, so a Close→Open cycle (or reconnect) never
438		// wedges on a stale high-water outer-seq.
439		m.resequencer.Store(reseq.New(resequencerWindow, resequencerTimeout, reseq.SystemClock{}))
440	
441		// Fresh FEC send/receive state per Open, when FEC is enabled (T24). The encoder
442		// group state and the decoder's per-group buffers re-pin with the sockets, so a
443		// Close→Open cycle never reconstructs against a stale group. Both are torn down in
444		// closeSocketsLocked. A build error here is a programmer error (the ratio was
445		// validated in NewMultipath), so it fails the Open.
446		if m.fecCfg != nil {
447			enc, err := fec.NewEncoder(*m.fecCfg, fec.SystemClock{})
448			if err != nil {
449				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
450				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: build FEC encoder: %w", err)
451			}
452			dec, err := fec.NewDecoder(*m.fecCfg)
453			if err != nil {
454				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
455				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: build FEC decoder: %w", err)
456			}
457			dec.SetRetainWindow(fecRetainGroups)
458			// Quiescence-accurate unrecoverable accounting (D24): fold a doomed group into
459			// the unrecoverable counter once its recovery could no longer have helped, even
460			// when no newer group arrives to advance the retain-window eviction (a stalled
461			// link, or traffic that simply stops after an incident). Past fecRecoveryDeadline
462			// from a group's first-seen instant, its deadline-flushed parity has certainly
463			// arrived (encoder grouping deadline) AND the resequencer has certainly skipped
464			// its gap (resequencerTimeout), so any later reconstruction is delivered too late
465			// to matter — the group is definitively unrecoverable and safe to count.
466			dec.SetClock(fec.SystemClock{})
467			dec.SetRecoveryDeadline(m.fecCfg.Deadline + resequencerTimeout)
468			m.fecSend = &fecSender{enc: enc}
469			// Adaptive mode (T29): a fresh controller per Open, re-pinned with the encoder so a
470			// Close→Open cycle starts the control law from M=0 (no standing redundancy until
471			// loss is observed) rather than inheriting a stale target. The controller cfg was
472			// validated in NewMultipath, so a build error here is a programmer error and fails
473			// the Open. The receive-side residual-loss estimator (post-FEC-recovery loss, the
474			// P4 signal) is likewise fresh per span.
475			if m.adaptiveCfg != nil {
476				ctrl, err := adaptivefec.NewController(*m.adaptiveCfg, adaptivefec.SystemClock{})
477				if err != nil {
478					_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
479					return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: build adaptive FEC controller: %w", err)
480				}
481				m.fecSend.ctrl = ctrl
482				// Adopt the controller's starting parity (0 — no standing redundancy until loss
483				// is observed) so the encoder and controller agree from t=0; the tick loop then
484				// sizes it to measured loss within the first control interval. Fixed mode leaves
485				// the encoder at its cfg.ParityShards default instead.
486				enc.SetParity(ctrl.Parity())
487			}
488			m.fecRecv.Store(&fecReceiver{dec: dec, connLoss: telemetry.NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)})
489		}
490	
491		// Resolve every path's interface up front and decide, per path, whether its
492		// socket may be device-bound (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard — survives a T16
493		// re-roam) or must pin the specific source IP (the pre-T16 behaviour, required
494		// when paths share an interface or the interface is multi-address, so distinct
495		// specific-IP sockets coexist on one port without an EADDRINUSE collision). See
496		// selectDeviceBinds.
497		srcs := make([]netip.Addr, len(m.defs))
498		for i := range m.defs {
499			srcs[i] = m.defs[i].SourceAddr
500		}
501		bindDevs := planPathBinds(srcs)
502	
503		// A path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr is merely NOT-YET-ASSIGNABLE at boot
504		// (EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the address — a 5G modem without a DHCP
505		// lease, Starlink mid-obstruction) is DEFERRED rather than treated as fatal, so
506		// the bond comes up on the paths that DO bind (G2/W1, approach A). Tolerating it
507		// requires the runtime path-down machinery: the deferred path's prober stays
508		// StateDown so the scheduler excludes it, and a DynamicScheduler whose membership
509		// can be reconciled to only the bound paths. Absent the probe transport (the T12
510		// no-prober unit binds) or a dynamic scheduler there is no such Down model, so —
511		// exactly as AddPath refuses a runtime add without probers — every bind error
512		// stays fatal there. A MALFORMED source_addr never reaches here (config.validate
513		// rejects it at load), and any OTHER bind error (EADDRINUSE, permission) is fatal.
514		dyn, dynOK := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler)
515		tolerateDefer := m.probers != nil && dynOK
516		m.deferred = nil
517	
518		actualPort := port
519		firstBound := true
520		// boundProbers is index-aligned with m.paths (the paths that actually bound), so
521		// the scheduler reconcile below and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping stay aligned
522		// even when a middle path deferred. nil in the no-prober case (nothing to reconcile).
523		var boundProbers []*telemetry.Prober
524		for i := range m.defs {
525			def := m.defs[i]
526			// Device-bind this path when selectDeviceBinds proved it safe (so a
527			// mid-session source-address change / T16 re-roam does not break the socket),
528			// otherwise pin the specific source IP. See selectDeviceBinds / listenPath.
529			c, err := listenPath(def.SourceAddr, port, bindDevs[i])
530			if err != nil {
531				if tolerateDefer && errors.Is(err, syscall.EADDRNOTAVAIL) {
532					// Defer this path: record its def + boot prober (kept Down) for the T55
533					// background reconcile to retry, and leave the bond to come up on the rest.
534					// Mirrors AddPath's rollback discipline — a failed path never disturbs the
535					// tunnel — at the boot boundary.
536					m.deferred = append(m.deferred, deferredPath{def: def, prober: m.probers[i]})
537					continue
538				}
539				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
540				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: open path %q on %s: %w", def.Name, def.SourceAddr, err)
541			}
542			// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort: the kernel caps at net.core.rmem_max and
543			// SetReadBuffer does not require privilege, so a returned error is rare;
544			// treat it as non-fatal rather than refusing to bind in a restricted env.
545			_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
546	
547			codec, err := frame.NewCodec(m.psk)
548			if err != nil {
549				_ = c.Close()
550				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
551				return nil, 0, err
552			}
553	
554			ps := &pathState{name: def.Name, id: uint8(i), src: def.SourceAddr, conn: c, codec: codec}
555			if m.probers != nil {
556				ps.prober = m.probers[i]
557				// Reconcile the DATA-frame path-id to the prober's IMMUTABLE stamp rather than
558				// to the slice index i: after a runtime RemovePath the survivor keeps its
559				// original (higher) stamp, so index-based numbering would renumber a live path
560				// AND diverge its DATA id from its PROBE stamp. Taking id from the prober keeps
561				// DATA and PROBE agreeing on the wire and a survivor's id stable across a reopen.
562				ps.id = ps.prober.PathID()
563				boundProbers = append(boundProbers, ps.prober)
564			}
565			switch {
566			case def.DestAddr.IsValid():
567				ps.setRemote(def.DestAddr)
568			case m.hasDefaultRemote:
569				ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
570			}
571			m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
572			if firstBound {
573				actualPort = uint16(c.LocalAddr().(*net.UDPAddr).Port)
574				firstBound = false
575			}
576		}
577	
578		// Hard guard (a): a bond with NO transport is impossible. If every configured path
579		// deferred, tolerance must NOT degrade to a zero-path bind — fail fatally, exactly
580		// as the pre-tolerance Open did when the sole path could not bind.
581		if len(m.paths) == 0 {
582			_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
583			return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: no configured path could bind its source address (all %d deferred as not-yet-assignable)", len(m.deferred))
584		}
585	
586		// nextPathID is a MONOTONIC HIGH-WATER carried ACROSS Open spans, never decreased.
587		// Close does not reset it, and here it is only ever RAISED to at least max(existing
588		// path stamp)+1. Resetting it to len(m.paths) (the pre-fix behaviour) re-minted an id
589		// a survivor still held once a RemovePath had opened a gap in the stamp space below
590		// that survivor's stamp: the next runtime AddPath then collided with the live path at
591		// an identical (PathID, SessionID), and because the peer's Reflector keys anti-replay
592		// AND the session challenge PER PathID, the strict-monotonic replay filter dropped one
593		// of the two independent ProbeSeq streams -> probe loss / false-DOWN. Deriving the
594		// high-water from the (prober-stamped) ps.id covers both the probe-transport case and
595		// the T12 no-prober case (where ps.id == i, so the high-water is len(m.paths)).
596		m.openPort = port
597		for _, ps := range m.paths {
598			if uint16(ps.id)+1 > m.nextPathID {
599				m.nextPathID = uint16(ps.id) + 1
600			}
601		}
602		// A DEFERRED path's boot prober already OWNS its path-id stamp; raise the
603		// high-water past it too, so a runtime AddPath arriving while the path is still
604		// deferred cannot mint that reserved id and collide with it once T55 binds it.
605		for _, dp := range m.deferred {
606			if dp.prober != nil {
607				if uint16(dp.prober.PathID())+1 > m.nextPathID {
608					m.nextPathID = uint16(dp.prober.PathID()) + 1
609				}
610			}
611		}
612		m.sendCodec = sendCodec
613	
614		// Reconcile the scheduler's membership with the path slice just rebuilt from
615		// m.defs. A runtime AddPath/RemovePath (T30) keeps m.defs, m.probers, AND the
616		// live scheduler in lockstep during an Open span; re-pinning the scheduler's
617		// health list to the BOUND probers HERE — index-aligned with m.paths, since each
618		// bound path appended its ps.prober to boundProbers in order above — is the single
619		// reconciliation point that makes that runtime membership survive this
620		// Close→Open cycle without a scheduler/path desync (no frozen zombie health
621		// entry, no resurrected removed path). A DEFERRED path (not-yet-assignable
622		// source_addr) is deliberately EXCLUDED here: its prober is absent from the
623		// scheduler, so Pick can never select it, and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping
624		// stays index-aligned with the bound-only path slice. Only meaningful with the
625		// probe transport: a bind without probers cannot change membership at runtime
626		// (AddPath is refused) and never defers, so its scheduler is left exactly as built.
627		if m.probers != nil && dynOK {
628			health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(boundProbers))
629			for i, pr := range boundProbers {
630				health[i] = pr
631			}
632			if err := dyn.SetPaths(health); err != nil {
633				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
634				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: reconcile scheduler on open: %w", err)
635			}
636		}
637	
638		// Stand up the receive fan-in: one Bind-owned reader per path feeding the shared
639		// resequencer, and a single engine-facing drainer. Both channels are recreated
640		// per Open so a Close→Open cycle starts clean.
641		m.deliverSignal = make(chan struct{}, 1)
642		m.recvClosed = make(chan struct{})
643		for _, ps := range m.paths {
644			m.readersWG.Add(1)
645			go m.readLoop(ps, m.deliverSignal)
646		}
647		// Deadline-tick goroutine for FEC group close (T24): it flushes a partial group's
648		// parity on time under low load so a group is never stranded waiting for the size
649		// threshold. Tracked by readersWG (like the readers) so Close waits for it; it
650		// exits on recvClosed. It TryLocks m.mu, so it never blocks Close's readersWG.Wait
651		// held under m.mu.
652		if m.fecSend != nil {
653			m.readersWG.Add(1)
654			go m.fecTickLoop(m.fecCfg.Deadline, m.recvClosed)
655		}
656		return []ReceiveFunc{m.newReceiveFunc(m.deliverSignal, m.recvClosed)}, actualPort, nil
657	}
658	
659	// readLoop is one Bind-OWNED per-path receive goroutine (T30). It reads the path
660	// socket and dispatches every datagram through handleInbound — which pushes DATA
661	// into the shared resequencer and answers/consumes PROBEs — then pokes the delivery
662	// signal so the single engine-facing drainer wakes to release any newly in-order
663	// frame. The read buffer is private to this goroutine, so the per-path Codec's
664	// scratch is never shared.
665	//
666	// Owning the readers in the Bind (rather than returning one ReceiveFunc per path to
667	// the engine, as T12 did) is what makes a runtime path add/remove possible without
668	// disturbing the engine: the engine builds its receive goroutines ONCE from Open's
669	// return, so a path added later gets a reader HERE while the engine's fixed receive
670	// set — and thus the WG session — sees no churn. The goroutine exits when its socket
671	// is closed (RemovePath drains one path; Close drains all).
672	func (m *Multipath) readLoop(ps *pathState, deliver chan<- struct{}) {
673		defer m.readersWG.Done()
674		readBuf := make([]byte, maxDatagram)
675		for {
676			n, srcAP, err := ps.conn.ReadFromUDPAddrPort(readBuf)
677			if err != nil {
678				return // socket closed: this path was removed, or the bind was closed
679			}
680			// Per-path received-wire accounting (T23): count the OUTER datagram this path
681			// pulled off its socket before dispatch, lock-free. This goroutine is the sole
682			// writer of ps.rxBytes, so the atomic Add is uncontended.
683			ps.rxBytes.Add(uint64(n))
684			m.handleInbound(ps, readBuf[:n], srcAP)
685			// Advance liveness off the receive path (throttled): a live signal on THIS path
686			// is what lets a DIFFERENT, silent path be marked DOWN promptly even when the
687			// probe-loop ticker is starved under load (D15). The peer probes every path
688			// (and floods the survivor after it roams), so the surviving path's reader
689			// supplies exactly that signal.
690			m.tickLivenessFromReceive(time.Now())
691			// Non-blocking poke: a single buffered slot coalesces bursts; the drainer
692			// re-checks the resequencer on every wake, so a coalesced poke loses nothing.
693			select {
694			case deliver <- struct{}{}:
695			default:
696			}
697		}
698	}
699	
700	// tickLivenessFromReceive re-evaluates EVERY path's liveness from a receive
701	// goroutine, throttled to at most once per probe interval (T39, defect D15). It is
702	// the starvation-robust companion to StartProbeLoop's timer-driven Tick: the timer
703	// goroutine can be delayed ~1s under CPU saturation, but the receive goroutines are
704	// scheduled by the very traffic that must trigger failover, so liveness advances
705	// regardless. Ticking is monotone-safe: Tick only marks an UP path DOWN once its
706	// silence STRICTLY exceeds DownAfter and never brings a path UP (that needs
707	// RecordEcho), so a more frequent Tick can only make a genuine DOWN transition land
708	// sooner — never a premature/false one — and the failback hysteresis (owned by the
709	// scheduler) is untouched. No-op when the probe transport is absent (interval unset).
710	func (m *Multipath) tickLivenessFromReceive(now time.Time) {
711		interval := m.sweepIntervalNanos.Load()
712		if interval == 0 {
713			return // no probe transport / loop not started: nothing to Tick
714		}
715		n := now.UnixNano()
716		last := m.lastSweepNanos.Load()
717		if n-last < interval {
718			return // throttled: this interval's sweep was already taken
719		}
720		if !m.lastSweepNanos.CompareAndSwap(last, n) {
721			return // another receive goroutine won this interval's sweep
722		}
723		// Snapshot the prober set under m.mu (a runtime AddPath/RemovePath mutates it),
724		// then Tick OUTSIDE the lock so a transition's log write never runs under m.mu.
725		// TryLock, NOT Lock: Close holds m.mu WHILE it waits on readersWG for the readers to
726		// exit, so a reader that BLOCKED here on m.mu would deadlock that shutdown. The sweep
727		// is opportunistic and throttled, so when the lock is contended (a concurrent
728		// Close/AddPath/RemovePath/Send) simply skipping this interval is harmless — the
729		// probe-loop ticker and the next receive still advance liveness. This preserves
730		// Close's invariant that a reader never blocks on m.mu. The lock, when taken, is held
731		// at most once per interval (~5/s) for a bounded snapshot, so it adds negligible
732		// contention to Send and does not disturb the lock-free receive fast path.
733		if !m.mu.TryLock() {
734			return
735		}
736		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, 0, len(m.paths))
737		for _, ps := range m.paths {
738			if ps.prober != nil {
739				probers = append(probers, ps.prober)
740			}
741		}
742		m.mu.Unlock()
743		for _, pr := range probers {
744			pr.Tick()
745		}
746		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18, the repeated-flap wedge). The active-backup
747		// selection is otherwise recomputed ONLY inside the scheduler's Pick, which the
748		// Bind calls ONLY from Send — so failover is pull-based: it happens only when the
749		// engine hands down an egress datagram. When the ACTIVE path dies during an egress
750		// LULL that is fatal: a repeated-flap kill landing on the just-restored primary
751		// before the saturating flow re-fills it (the failback and the next kill overlap)
752		// leaves both TCP directions stalled on the now-dead path, so NO Send occurs, so
753		// Pick is never called, so egress is never switched to the healthy backup — the
754		// bond wedges on the dead path until the 25s WG keepalive finally drives a Send.
755		// The receive tick is the starvation-robust signal that ALREADY detected the DOWN
756		// (it just Ticked the path down above); recomputing the scheduler from it makes the
757		// switch EAGER — bounded by the detection window (~DownAfter + one interval) rather
758		// than by the next application Send. Pick recomputes purely against current liveness
759		// and the clock and the failback dwell is time-based, so a more frequent recompute
760		// can only make a genuine transition land sooner — never a premature/false failover,
761		// and never a shortened anti-thrash hysteresis. It takes ONLY the scheduler's own
762		// lock (never m.mu), so it adds no receive-path m.mu contention and cannot invert
763		// the Send-path m.mu→scheduler lock order.
764		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()
765	}
766	
767	// nudgeSchedulerActive forces the scheduler to recompute its active egress path
768	// against current liveness, independent of any application Send. It is the eager-
769	// failover companion to the Send-driven Pick: driven from the liveness-detection
770	// paths (the receive tick and the probe loop) it switches egress to a healthy backup
771	// the moment the active path is detected DOWN, so a dead active path with stalled
772	// egress does not wedge the bond until the next Send (defect D18). The returned index
773	// is intentionally discarded — only the recompute (and its logged transition) is
774	// wanted here; Send remains the sole reader of the selection for actual routing. Pick
775	// is internally synchronized and never calls back into the Bind, so this is safe to
776	// call from a receive goroutine or the probe loop without m.mu.
777	//
778	// It calls Recompute, NOT Pick: the nudge wants only the liveness-driven active-set
779	// recompute (and its logged transition), and a weighted/aggregating scheduler's Pick
780	// is STATEFUL — a spurious Pick here would consume a distribution slot and skew the
781	// per-path weight split. Recompute is the non-consuming half that refreshes the
782	// eligible/active set without touching distribution/pacing/load state, so the T40
783	// eager-failover guarantee holds for BOTH the active-backup and the weighted policy
784	// (defect D18).
785	func (m *Multipath) nudgeSchedulerActive() {
786		m.scheduler.Recompute()
787	}
788	
789	// newReceiveFunc returns the SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc: it drains the shared
790	// resequencer in outer-seq order and hands each inner datagram up under the one
791	// virtual endpoint. Because every path's reader feeds this one drainer, a path added
792	// or removed at runtime needs no change to the engine's receive set. When nothing is
793	// ready it parks until a reader pokes deliver, until Close closes closed, or until a
794	// short poll elapses — the poll guarantees a head-of-line-blocked run still makes
795	// timeout progress even if the last live path fell silent right after buffering it.
796	// A single drainer also delivers with ZERO added reorder (only it calls Pop), which
797	// is stricter than T12's per-path receivers.
798	func (m *Multipath) newReceiveFunc(deliver <-chan struct{}, closed <-chan struct{}) ReceiveFunc {
799		// One reusable poll timer per drainer, NOT a fresh time.After timer per park: the
800		// drainer parks on every empty receive, and a per-park 250 ms timer — retained by
801		// the runtime timer heap until it fires even after the park is over — added
802		// avoidable allocation and GC pressure on the receive hot path (opus low defect).
803		// The ReceiveFunc is driven by a single engine goroutine, so the timer has no
804		// concurrent user; Stop+drain then Reset per park reuses the one allocation.
805		timer := time.NewTimer(resequencerTimeout)
806		stopTimer := func() {
807			timer.Stop()
808			select {
809			case <-timer.C:
810			default:
811			}
812		}
813		stopTimer() // start inert; armed only while parked
814		return func(packets [][]byte, sizes []int, eps []Endpoint) (int, error) {
815			for {
816				// Deliver any in-order resequenced DATA. The item carries the outer source
817				// of the frame that produced it, so the virtual endpoint pins correctly even
818				// when the frame was buffered and released out of arrival order.
819				if it, ok := m.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
820					if len(it.Payload) > len(packets[0]) {
821						continue // oversize inner datagram: drop, keep draining
822					}
823					sizes[0] = copy(packets[0], it.Payload)
824					eps[0] = m.virtualEndpoint(it.Src)
825					return 1, nil
826				}
827				timer.Reset(resequencerTimeout)
828				select {
829				case <-deliver:
830					stopTimer()
831				case <-timer.C:
832					// Poll fired; its channel is already drained by this receive.
833				case <-closed:
834					stopTimer()
835					return 0, errClosed
836				}
837			}
838		}
839	}
840	
841	// handleInbound decodes one received outer datagram and dispatches it by kind. It
842	// is the single per-frame receive action, factored out of readLoop so the probe
843	// transport is exercisable without spinning receive goroutines. Delivery up the WG
844	// path is deferred to the resequencer (Pop, in the engine-facing drainer): a DATA
845	// frame is not handed up here but pushed into the shared resequencer to be released in
846	// outer-seq order.
847	//
848	//   - DATA: the decoded inner datagram is pushed into the resequencer keyed by
849	//     the frame's outer-seq (delivered later, in order, via Pop). The path's
850	//     remote is NOT learned here — remote-learning is authenticated-only (see
851	//     below), so a forged DATA frame cannot steer a path's return endpoint.
852	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=false: an authenticated peer probe. Its source is learned as
853	//     the path's remote (D11: a probe-only backup path gets a usable return remote
854	//     before any DATA flows) and it is reflected straight back to that source via
855	//     this path's socket (T13 Reflector). Reflection writes independently of the
856	//     scheduler/getRemote so an echo returns even on a not-yet-active path.
857	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=true: an authenticated echo of one of our own probes. Its
858	//     source is learned as the remote too, and the raw echo is fed into this
859	//     path's Prober (HandleEcho) to update RTT/loss and drive liveness.
860	//   - anything else (PARITY/CONTROL/malformed): dropped.
861	//
862	// Remote-learning and reflection touch only authenticated (MAC-verified) PROBE
863	// frames — Decode has already verified the tag for the PROBE kind — which is what
864	// resolves D9: an attacker who forges an unauthenticated DATA frame can no longer
865	// repoint a path's return endpoint.
866	//
867	// FEC seam (T24): DATA ingestion via resequencer.Observe is keyed purely on
868	// outer-seq, so an FEC decoder can slot in BEFORE the resequencer with no change
869	// here — PARITY (dropped today) will feed the decoder, which reconstructs missing
870	// DATA frames and calls Observe with their ORIGINAL outer-seq, identical to a
871	// natively-received frame.
872	func (m *Multipath) handleInbound(ps *pathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
873		fr, err := ps.codec.Decode(raw)
874		if err != nil {
875			return // drop malformed / PSK-mismatched outer frames
876		}
877		switch f := fr.(type) {
878		case frame.Data:
879			// Decode already returned a fresh copy of the payload (it aliases nothing
880			// else), so the resequencer may take ownership of it directly.
881			rq := m.resequencer.Load()
882			if fr := m.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
883				// FEC on (T24): offer the data shard to the decoder BEFORE resequencing so a
884				// later parity frame can reconstruct any group-mate lost in transit, then
885				// deliver THIS received frame in its own right (the decoder never echoes a
886				// directly-received data shard back). The shard's coded bytes are
887				// OuterSeq || Payload — the same bytes the sender coded parity over.
888				shard := fec.DataShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.FECIndex), Payload: fecShardPayload(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload)}
889				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
890				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
891				// Residual-loss accounting (T29): this outer-seq was natively delivered, so mark
892				// it present in the post-recovery loss estimator. A seq never marked here nor via
893				// a reconstruction below is loss that FEC did not mask.
894				if fr.connLoss != nil {
895					fr.connLoss.Observe(f.OuterSeq)
896				}
897				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
898			} else {
899				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
900			}
901		case frame.Parity:
902			// PARITY feeds the FEC decoder (T24); a group that has now accumulated enough
903			// shards reconstructs its missing data frames, each resequenced at its ORIGINAL
904			// outer-seq (carried in the recovered shard's coded bytes) so recovery composes
905			// with T18 exactly like a natively-received frame. With FEC off, PARITY is
906			// dropped (the pre-T24 behaviour).
907			if fr := m.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
